```html Git Cheatsheet

What is Git?

Git - version control system

What is GitHub?

GitHub - database to store code

Git Basics

  • git init - initialize a git repository
  • git status - tells us what is happening in the project
  • git add . - add the changes in the "to be committed" list
  • git restore --staged - to remove a file from the "to be committed" list
  • git commit -m "message" - commits the changes to git history
  • git log - check all the commits made
  • git reset - take your project back to that commit (all commits after that are deleted)

Git Stash

  • git stash - to hide the changes you made (for example trying something new in your code and you want to remove the files but also don't want to commit them)
  • git stash pop - reverting all the changes you made in the files you stashed
  • git stash clear - to remove the changes permanently

Git Remote & Pushing

  • git remote add origin - to attach a GitHub repo to a local project
  • git push origin master - push changes to GitHub URL you attached your project with

Branching and Merging

  • git branch feature - create a new branch called feature
  • git checkout feature - change branch to feature (i.e., all the commits will be on the feature branch and the main branch will be left as it is)
  • git merge feature - now feature and main branch are merged

Forking and Rebasing

  • git remote add upstream - upstream is the project from where you have forked the project and origin is your personal project
  • git fetch --all --prune - to get all the fetches from upstream
  • git reset --hard upstream/master - sync your local master with upstream master
  • git pull upstream master - same as fetch and reset we did above
  • git rebase -i - to squash commits into one commit

Pro Tips

  • Add a .gitignore file to ignore files/folders you don’t want to track (like node_modules/, .env, etc.).
  • Always use meaningful commit messages.
```
